items.title: cd – Change Directory (and Its Variations)
items.body: Everyone learns cd to change directories - but its lesser-known variations can save you a ton of back-and-forth typing.

snippets.label: cd
snippets.detail: With no argument, jumps straight to your home directory.

snippets.label: cd ..
snippets.detail: Moves up one directory level, to the parent folder.

snippets.label: cd ~
snippets.detail: Jumps straight to your home directory, no matter where you currently are.

snippets.label: cd -
snippets.detail: Jumps back to the previous directory you were just in — a quick way to toggle between two folders.

snippets.label: cd /
snippets.detail: Jumps straight to the filesystem root.

items.excerpt: Beyond basic `cd`, shortcuts like `cd -`, `cd ..`, and `cd ~` jump between directories instantly and cut out repetitive typing.

items.title: 10 Essential Terminal Commands
items.body: The classics everyone should know cold - these ten commands cover most of what you'll do in a terminal on any given day.

snippets.label: pwd
snippets.detail: Prints the full path of the directory you're currently in.

snippets.label: ls
snippets.detail: Lists the files and folders in your current directory.

snippets.label: cd
snippets.detail: Changes your current directory to the one you specify.

snippets.label: mkdir
snippets.detail: Creates a new, empty directory with the name you give it.

snippets.label: rm
snippets.detail: Removes (deletes) files. Add -r to remove whole folders — there's no trash bin, so use it carefully.

snippets.label: cp
snippets.detail: Copies a file (or folder, with -r) to a new location, leaving the original in place.

snippets.label: mv
snippets.detail: Moves a file to a new location, or renames it if the destination is in the same folder.

snippets.label: cat
snippets.detail: Prints a file's entire contents straight to the terminal window.

snippets.label: grep
snippets.detail: Searches for a pattern of text inside files and prints the matching lines.

snippets.label: sudo
snippets.detail: Runs the next command with admin (superuser) privileges — use it when a command says 'permission denied.'

items.excerpt: Ten essential terminal commands covering navigation, files, and processes — the core commands you'll use nearly every day.

items.title: pwd – Where Am I?
items.body: Not sure which folder you're in? `pwd` prints the full path of your current working directory.
items.excerpt: `pwd` prints the full path of your current directory in the terminal — the fastest way to confirm exactly where you are before running a command.

items.title: Terminal Cheat Cards
items.body: A handful of everyday terminal commands worth keeping at your fingertips  quick reference, not deep dives.

snippets.label: ls
snippets.detail: Lists what's inside the current folder.

snippets.label: cd ..
snippets.detail: Moves up one directory level.

snippets.label: mkdir
snippets.detail: Creates a new directory.

snippets.label: clear
snippets.detail: Wipes the terminal screen so you're looking at a clean slate.

snippets.label: history
snippets.detail: Shows a list of commands you've previously run in this session.

items.excerpt: A quick-reference set of everyday terminal commands — ls, cd, pwd, and more — for a fast reminder, not a full tutorial.

items.title: How to Initiate a Node.js Project
items.body: Before running `npm install` in a brand-new project folder, you need to initialize it first. Run `npm init -y` to generate a default `package.json` file - without it, npm has nowhere to record your dependencies.

snippets.label: npm init -y
snippets.detail: Creates a package.json with default values, skipping the interactive prompts, so npm has a place to track your project's dependencies.

items.excerpt: Run `npm init -y` before `npm install` in a new project folder — it generates the `package.json` file npm needs to track dependencies.
